title: BES Cyber System Impact Rating Drift Detection

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-002-6 R2.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-002-6 R2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.2: BES Cyber System Impact Rating Drift Detection.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.2' running on index=ot_security asset records, bes_cyber_system_categorization.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=ot_security` asset records, `bes_cyber_system_categorization.csv` — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151) (Splunkbase)
spl:
| inputlookup bes_cyber_system_categorization.csv
| eval key=lower(bes_asset_hostname)
| join type=left key [
    search index=ot_security sourcetype="ot:asset" earliest=-24h
    | eval key=lower(hostname)
    | stats latest(impact_tag) as observed_impact by key
  ]
| where lower(observed_impact)!=lower(impact_rating)
| table bes_system, impact_rating, observed_impact, key
description: We highlight when operational tags on hosts disagree with the registered high or medium impact rating so impact determinations stay defensible.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes ot_security and sourcetypes ot:asset. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Populate `impact_tag` from OT Security Add-on or CMDB sync. (2) Tune mapping for staging assets. (3) Monthly governance report. (4) Drive formal CIP-002 review updates when drift persists. Indexes required: `index=ot_security`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=ot:asset`; Lookups: `bes_cyber_system_categorization`, `bes_cyber_system_categorization.csv`.
visualization: Table (mismatches), Single value (drift count), Column chart (by site). Key fields: `bes_system`, `impact_rating`, `key`, `observed_impact`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Planned generation outage maintenance** — Scheduled maintenance outages on BES Cyber Systems require physical and electronic access that triggers CIP access monitoring alerts; these are pre-approved via the outage management system and documented in the maintenance plan.

2. **NERC compliance audit field activities** — During triennial CIP compliance audits, Regional Entity auditors perform physical walkthroughs, system sampling, and configuration reviews that generate access and configuration query events.

3. **Relay and protection system testing** — Periodic relay testing, protection coordination studies, and SCADA system communication tests produce legitimate BES operational signals that are scheduled and documented in the maintenance programme.

4. **Vendor remote access for approved patches** — ICS/SCADA vendors granted time-limited interactive remote access (IRA) through the jump host for approved firmware updates and configuration changes are covered by the vendor risk management programme.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `nerc_cip_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved outage tickets, audit period dates, relay test schedule entries, and vendor IRA session IDs. The search filters against this lookup and suppresses events where `cip_exception_approved=true` and the window is active.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `ot_security` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `bes_cyber_system_categorization.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('bes_cyber_system_categorization.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `CIP-002-6 R2`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-002-6 R2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.2: BES Cyber System Impact Rating Drift Detection." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `ot_security` under `ot:asset`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Populate `impact_tag` from OT Security Add-on or CMDB sync. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** Tune mapping for staging assets. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 3:** Monthly governance report. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 4:** Drive formal CIP-002 review updates when drift persists. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "BES Cyber System Impact Rating Drift Detection," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NERC CIP control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`ot_security`, sourcetype=`ot:asset`) with a subsearch on `key` across the last 24 hours. `type=left` preserves all rows from the outer search; nulls in the inner side indicate missing data that may itself constitute a finding.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.13.2: BES Cyber System Impact Rating Drift Detection]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.13.2,reg=NERC_CIP
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=ot_security sourcetype=ot:asset earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `bes_system`, `impact_rating`, `key`, `observed_impact`. Run:

```spl
index=ot_security sourcetype=ot:asset earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table bes_system, impact_rating, key, observed_impact
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.13.2: NERC CIP evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`mismatches`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`drift count`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'NERC CIP current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Distribution (`by site`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NERC CIP-specific failure modes:**

• BES Cyber System classification (CIP-002): every NERC CIP detection must filter to BES Cyber Systems only. A general 'Windows login' search across the entire estate is not CIP evidence; a search filtered through `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv` is.

• Low-Impact vs Medium-Impact vs High-Impact: CIP requirements scale with impact rating. A detection that fires identically for Low and High-Impact systems over-applies controls (wastes IR effort) and under-applies audit attention. Include impact rating in the alert payload and route by rating.

• ERC (Electronic Security Perimeter) boundary: CIP-005 requires strict ESP documentation. Firewall-rule detections must reference the ESP boundary register — a rule change on a firewall that's not an EAP is NOT a CIP finding; a rule change on an EAP is.

• CIP-010 configuration baselines: baseline monitoring must use an approved baseline, not an informal one. Reference the CIP-010 attestation register in the UC; baselines without dated attestation signatures are not regulatory evidence.
grandmaExplanation: We highlight when operational tags on hosts disagree with the registered high or medium impact rating so impact determinations stay defensible. That is important for the big power grid, where proof needs to line up with what the rules expect.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'BES Cyber System Impact Rating Drift Detection' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper NERC CIP control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the NERC CIP control objective.

evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with NERC CIP control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the NERC CIP programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
